explanation: The sun warms some patches of ground more than the ones beside them. The air above a warm patch grows lighter and drifts upward. Cooler air from nearby slides across to fill the gap it leaves. That steady flow of air brushing past you is the wind.

phrasing: What makes icebergs stay on the surface of the water?
explanation: An iceberg is a huge chunk of ice that broke off from a glacier. Ice forms when water gets cold enough to freeze solid. As water freezes, it spreads apart and takes up a little more space. That spreading makes the ice weigh less than the same amount of water. Anything lighter than water will bob up instead of sinking. Only the top peeks out, while most of the iceberg hides below.

phrasing: How can fish see when they're in the water?
explanation: A fish has eyes shaped to focus light while it swims. Sunlight from above filters down and drifts through the water. The light bounces off rocks, plants, and other fish nearby. Some of that bounced light lands inside the fish's eyes. Its eyes pass the picture back to its brain to read.

phrasing: Can you explain what photosynthesis means?
explanation: A green plant builds its own food out of sunlight. The green stuff in its leaves grabs the light that falls on them. The plant pulls in air and drinks up water through its roots. It blends the light, air, and water into a kind of sugar. That sugar feeds the plant so it can grow taller and stronger.

phrasing: How come we have tears when we're sad?
explanation: Small glands above each eye keep making a thin, salty fluid. A rush of strong feeling tells those glands to pour out more of it. The extra fluid brims over your eyelids and slides down as tears. Letting it out often leaves you feeling a bit calmer inside.

A very thin film of water always coats the top of the ice. Your foot presses on that film and skates across it with ease. Almost nothing on the smooth surface can catch and hold your shoe. Your feet slide out from under you before you can stop them. That slick, watery coat is the reason ice trips people up.

The sun pours out strong rays that our eyes are unable to see. A little of this ray tans the skin, but too much harms it. The rays damage the tiny living cells in the top of your skin. Your body rushes blood to the spot to heal the hurt cells. That extra blood turns the skin red, hot, and sore to touch. A hat and some shade block the rays before they can burn you.

concept: How does a seed know when to grow?
phrasing: What tells a seed to start growing?
explanation: A seed can rest in the cold ground for a long while. It stays asleep until the world around it feels just right. Spring warmth and soaked-up water are the clues it waits for. Those clues switch the tiny plant inside the seed awake. A small root reaches down and a green shoot pushes up.

phrasing: What makes lightning appear during a storm?
explanation: A storm cloud is full of ice bits tumbling and bumping about. All that bumping rubs an electric charge onto the pieces. The charge piles up until the cloud can hold no more of it. It escapes in a flash, leaping through the air as a huge spark. That blazing spark tearing across the sky is the lightning you see.

concept: What do insects do in the winter?
phrasing: Can you tell me what insects do when it's winter?
explanation: Winter air turns too cold for most insects to buzz around. Many crawl under loose bark or dig down into the ground. Down there their bodies slow to a crawl and they drift into rest. They stay tucked away until spring warms the world again.

phrasing: Can you explain how we detect different smells?
explanation: Everything with a scent sheds tiny bits that float off into the air. When you breathe in, those bits ride the air up into your nose. High inside your nose sits a patch of special smelling cells. The floating bits stick to that patch and set the cells buzzing. The cells fire a quick signal along a nerve to your brain. Your brain reads the signal and names the smell for you.

concept: Why do we have to sleep?
phrasing: What is the reason we need sleep?
explanation: Your body and brain work without a break all day long. Sleep is the quiet time when they finally get to rest. While you doze, your body repairs sore muscles and worn parts. Your brain sifts through the day and files away what you learned. You wake refreshed, with a clearer head and fresh energy.

concept: How do bubbles form in boiling water?
phrasing: Can you tell me how bubbles start in hot water?
explanation: A pot on the stove heats the water sitting at its bottom first. When that water gets hot enough, it changes from liquid into gas. The gas cannot stay mixed in, so it clumps into tiny bubbles. Each bubble is lighter than the water pressing around it. The bubbles rise in a wobbly line toward the top of the pot. They burst at the surface, and that churning is the boil you watch.

concept: Why do stars appear in different patterns?
phrasing: Can you tell me why we see stars in various formations?
explanation: The night sky is dotted with stars scattered across deep space. From the ground they look like tiny sparks flung in every direction. Your eyes tend to join nearby sparks into shapes and outlines. People long ago traced those shapes and named them after animals. The patterns live in our minds, not in the stars themselves.

phrasing: Can you tell me how a volcano erupts?
explanation: Far below the ground, it is so hot that solid rock melts. The melted rock is lighter than the hard rock around it. Being lighter, it slowly rises toward the surface over time. It gathers in a pool and pushes hard against the rock above. Gas trapped inside the melt adds even more push from within. When the rock on top finally cracks, the melt bursts out. It pours down the slopes as glowing rivers we call lava.
